coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of carbon dots with mangosteen pulp for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Rui
Guo, Xiangfeng
Jia, Lihua
Zhang, Yu
Zhao, Zhenlong
Lonshakov, Fedor
coredata.dc:description: A simple method was developed in the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (referred to as M-CDs), calcined treatment of mangosteen pulp in air, without the assistance of any chemical reagent. The M-CDs possess good-solubility, satisfactory chemical stability and can be applied as the fluorescent temperature probe. More strikingly, the fluorescence of M-CDs can be fleetly and selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions. The phenomenon was used to develop a fluorescent method for facile detection of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0–0.18mM and a detection limit of 52nM. Eventually, the M-CDs were applied for cell imaging, demonstrating their potential toward diverse applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Mangosteen pulp
Fe3+ detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of M-CDs to Fe3+ and fluorescence quenching mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education of Heilongjiang Province of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21176125) and the Science Research Project of the Ministry of Education of Heilongjiang Province of China (135109101).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of green synthetic procedure of M-CDs for Fe3+ ions detections and cells imaging. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) TEM images of M-CDs, (b) the aggregates of M-CDs/Fe3+.
(a) Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of M-CDs. (b) Emission spectra of M-CDs at different excitation wavelength from 290 to 470nm. (The inset displays corresponding normalized spectra.).
Temperature effect on the fluorescence emission of M-CDs: (a) Cyclic test; (b) Linear temperature increase.

Selectivity of M-CDs (0.30mg/mL) for Fe3+ (0.40mM) over other metal ions in Tris buffer (pH 7.3, 10mM). The black bars represent the addition of metal ions (0.40mM for all the metal ions). The red bars represent the subsequent addition of 0.40mM Fe3+ to the M-CDs solution. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The fluorescence responses of M-CDs (0.30mg/mL) in Tris buffer (pH 7.3, 10mM) after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (Inset: plots of intensity I 440 versus the concentrations of Fe3+ (0–0.18mM)). (b) The Stern-Volmer plot of M-CDs at various concentrations of Fe3+ (0–0.28mM). (F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of M-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+).

The bright field (a) and fluorescence imaging (b) of the yeast cells labeled with the M-CDs; the bright field (c) and fluorescence imaging (d) of the yeast cells labeled with the M-CDs in the presence of 0.40mM Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of carbon dots with mangosteen pulp for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, College of Heilongjiang Province, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots were synthesized by a simple calcined method, using mangosteen pulp as carbon source, without the assistance of any chemical reagent. These M-CDs show strong blue fluorescence, which were quenched by Fe3+ and can be used for cell imaging.

A simple method was developed in the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots (referred to as M-CDs), calcined treatment of mangosteen pulp in air, without the assistance of any chemical reagent. The M-CDs possess good-solubility, satisfactory chemical stability and can be applied as the fluorescent temperature probe. More strikingly, the fluorescence of M-CDs can be fleetly and selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions. The phenomenon was used to develop a fluorescent method for facile detection of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0–0.18mM and a detection limit of 52nM. Eventually, the M-CDs were applied for cell imaging, demonstrating their potential toward diverse applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of nanomaterials in the nanocarbon family, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn tremendous attention in recent research [1]. CDs are usually spherical structure with the size below 10nm and they can be prepared by facile, simple and rapid synthetic methods with cheap and available raw materials [2]. Meanwhile, they possess super solubility in water, excellent biocompatibility, excellent optical property, low cytotoxicity, and resistance to photobleaching compared with heavy metal based quantum dots and organic dyes [3–5]. In this context, the wide potential of CDs has been exploited in the fields of bio-imaging [6,7], fluorescent ink [8], photo-catalysis [9], and sensor [10–13]. CDs are considered to be the next generation's green nanomaterials to replace conventionally used fluorescence probes, which was more suitable for many novel applications [14].

Nowadays, various green raw materials are used as carbon source for the fabrication of CDs. Besides, most raw materials possess complex component, so CDs made from these materials usual possess various surface groups and special performance [15]. It was reported that coconut in the mixed water/ethanol (1:1v/v) solution was used as carbon source for preparing carbon dots with a quantum yields of 2.8% [14]. Similarly, it was demonstrated the blueberries as carbon source synthesized CDs with liquid nitrogen (N2)-assisted [16]. The combustion of the sugarcane bagasse pulp in toluene solution was stirred thoroughly and continuously for 24h and synthesized CDs at room temperature [17]. Chocolate in NaOH solution was used to produce CDs in the polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle for 8h [2]. CDs have also been achieved by using pure honey as carbon source in the presence of H2O2 [18]. However, most of the preparations of CDs, the green materials often suffer from serious bottlenecking such as low quantum yield, assistance of gas, toxic organic solvents, time consuming process, required strong base environment. Therefore, much effort has been put into developing green routes for preparing CDs from raw materials which are widely available and harmless to the environment [19–21].

Fe3+, one of the most essential metal ions in biological systems, plays crucial roles in clinic and environment [22]. However, excess amounts of Fe3+ in environment and human body cause environmental pollution and some diseases. Thus, the determination of Fe3+ is of fundamental importance for the early identification and diagnosis of these diseases. In recent years, the considerable importance of Fe3+ in biological and environmental systems has led to increasing interest in the development of fluorescence CDs for the determination of Fe3+ [23–28]. Although these CDs exhibited good performance in Fe3+ detection, there is still much room to explore the new fluorescent Fe3+ sensing probe with high sensitivity and selectivity.

In the present work, we have developed a simple approach for preparation of M-CDs with mangosteen pulp as carbon source, without the assistance of any chemical reagent. It showed commendable potential in traces Fe3+ ions detection and sensitive temperature probe. As an effective probe for Fe3+, there was a good linear correlation between the quenching efficiency and the concentration of Fe3+ in a linear range from 0 to 0.18mM with a detection limit of 52nM. As temperature probe, the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs decreased linearly with increasing temperature from 10°C to 90°C with excellent sensitivity and recoverability. Meanwhile, the M-CDs were employed for cell imaging, demonstrating their potential in biomedical fields.

The mangosteen was purchased from local supermarket (Qiqihar, China). NaClO4·H2O, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, Cr(NO3)3 .FeCl3·6H2O, CoSO4·7H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, AgNO3, CdSO4·8H2O, HgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, and AlCl3·6H2O were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers (analytical grade). Ultra-pure water (18.2MΩcm) obtained from the Millipore system was used throughout all experiments.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were investigated by transmission electron micros-copy (Hitachi, H-7650, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were collected by Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo, America). ATR-IR spectral data were measured with Perkinelmer spotlight 400 (Perkinelmer, America). Fluorescence steady state spectra were obtained on a Hitachi F-7000 (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Puxi TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). Fluorescence imaging was carried out with a DSU live microscope system (Olympus, Japan). All pH measurements were made with a Sartorius basic pH-meter PB-10 (Göttingen, Germany).

Carbon dots were synthesized based on the procedure described in the literature [29]. In the paper, we choose using green raw materials mangosteen as carbon source instead of organic compounds. In detail, first, mangosteen peel was removed, and 5g mangosteen pulp was weighed. The mangosteen pulp was mashed into a paste in a ceramic crucible. Then the ceramic crucible was placed into an electric heating mantle about 10min until the color of the solids changed from white to yellowish-brown. The ceramic crucible was cooled down at room temperature and the clustered solids pounded into yellow-brown powder. And then, 10.0mL ultrapure water was injected into the ceramic crucible. After vigorous stirring and ultraphonic vibrating, the suspension solution was centrifuged at 9000rpm for 10min to dislodge the undissolved substance. The upper yellowish-brown solution is further purified through a 0.45μm membrane filter. The obtained solution was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MW=3500) for 24h. Finally, the M-CDs were vacuum-dried to a brown solid.

For the selectivity test towards Fe3+, the detection of Fe3+ was confirmed by adding other common metal ions stock solutions (including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe2+ and Al3+ ions) instead of Fe3+ and determining in the same way at pH 7.3 in Tris buffer (10mM). The concentration of M-CDs and these metal ions species were 0.30mg/mL and 0.40mM, respectively. The similar competition experiments were carried out by adding Fe3+ (0.40mM) to the solution of M-CDs (0.30mg/mL) in the presence of 0.40mM of the other metal ions.

To evaluate the sensitivity towards Fe3+, different concentration of Fe3+ (pH 7.3) was added into the aqueous solution of M-CDs (0.30mg/mL) and the mixed solution was stirred for 2min before spectral measurements. The fluorescence was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 330nm. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

The QY of M-CDs was determined according to an established procedure. In brief, quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY: 0.54 at 330nm) [30] was chose as the standard. The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ fl) was calculated using the relative method according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ fl = Φ s ×(F/F s)×(A s/A)×(n/n s)2
where Φ s is the reported quantum yield of the standard, F and Fs are the integrated area of sample and standard solutions, respectively, A and A s are the absorbance of sample and standard solutions, respectively, and n is the refractive index with 1.33 as the default for both quinine sulfate and M-CDs.

The yeast cells were cultured in YPD media (1.4% yeast extract, 4.0% peptone, 2.0% glucose, 0.2% (NH4)2SO4) and incubated for 24h at 37°C. Then, the yeast cells were collected and cultured with 0.30mg/mL M-CDs. After incubation for 1h, the cells were washed thoroughly three times with PBS (1mL each time) and kept in PBS for the fluorescence imaging. Thereafter, 0.40mM Fe3+ was added to the fluorescence-labeled cells, which was incubated for 30min. The fluorescence imaging of cell was recorded by using a fluorescence microscope.

The mangosteen pulp was used as carbon source, which contained large number of organic molecules (dietary fiber, amino acid, carbohydrates, vitamin and many more). Therefore, as prepared M-CDs fabricated from mangosteen pulp is easy to be functionalized. To further identify the structure of M-CDs, the synthesized M-CDs were characterised by TEM. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of M-CDs was shown in Fig. 1 a, which shows that the M-CDs had high dispersity and uniform spherical shapes, and the average diameter of the M-CDs was approximately 5nm. After upon addition of Fe3+ to the M-CDs system, formed the M-CDs/Fe3+ complex, appeared as aggregation morphology of several M-CDs with approximately 25nm in diameter (Fig. 1b). The result demonstrated that the M-CDs were aggregated in the presence of Fe3+.

In addition, the surface functional groups of M-CDs were investigated by FTIR spectra. As shown in Fig. S1. The broad band in the region of 3036–3625cm−1 arouse from stretching vibrations of OH and NH, The absorption peak at 2927cm−1 and 2887cm−1 is because of −CH vibrational stretching of −CH3 and −CH2. The absorption peak at 1716cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of CO [31]. The peaks at 1613 and 1405cm−1 indicated to the existence of COO− groups [32]. In addition, the absorption peak around 1019cm−1 could be assigned to the CO groups [14].

The surface composition of the synthesized M-CDs was examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S2a, the XPS spectrum of M-CDs displayed three typical peaks at 285.1, 401.1, and 532.1eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The XPS analysis of the elemental composition of M-CDs demonstrated that they were mainly composed of carbon (62.55%), nitrogen (3.16%) and oxygen (34.29%). The spectrum of C1s in Fig. S2b exhibited three main peaks. The binding energy peak at 284.7eV was attributed to CC bond, while the peak around 286.1eV could be assigned to COH, and the peak around 287.8eV could be ascribed to CO [33,34]. The N1s spectrum could be divided into two peaks (Fig. S2c). The main peak with the binding energy of 401.8eV was attributed to the NH. Another small peak at 399.7eV originated from CNC [35]. As shown in Fig. S2d, The spectrum of the O1s element indicated the shoulder peak of 533.94eV ascribed to the COH/COC groups [36].

The optical properties of M-CDs were explored in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a, In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the two characteristic absorption peaks are located at 283nm and 350nm. The peak at 283nm could be attributed to the π-π* transition for aromatic sp2 hybridisation [29], another peak at about 350nm was from n-π* transitions of the unsaturated groups with nitrogen atoms and resulted in strong emission due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states [37,38].

In addition, the fluorescence emission spectrum of M-CDs exhibited strong peak at 440nm. The M-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission character. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescent peak red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength from 290nm to 470nm, and the strongest emission spectrum was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 330nm. The inset of Fig. 2b displayed corresponding normalized spectra. These behaviors resulted from the different sizes of M-CDs and a distribution of different surface states due to the different organic groups on the surface of M-CDs [39].

Then, the different concentration of M-CDs was explored. As shown in Fig. S3a, the results showed that the best concentration of M-CDs was 0.30mg/mL. The influence of shelf time on the fluorescence stability of M-CDs was also evaluated. After two-month storage, the M-CDs were relatively stable that its fluorescence was slightly reduced less than 6% (Fig. S3b). Fig. S3c showed the fluorescence photostability of M-CDs under continuing Xe-lamp irradiation. The results showed the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs still kept >90% after a 3000s continuing irradiation.

Meanwhile, we also have investigated the effect of ionic strength (in terms of the concentration of KCl) on the fluorescence of M-CDs. As seen in Fig. S3d, different concentrations of KCl solutions range from 0M to 2M were formulated as the solvents for M-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of M-CDs remained almost unchanged with the KCl concentrations.

The effects of temperature on the fluorescence of M-CDs aqueous solutions were investigated. In the range from 10 to 90°C, When alternately changed the temperature of the M-CDs solution, it found that the fluorescence decreased at higher temperature (90°C) and the fluorescence was recovered when temperature back to 10°C (Fig. 3 a). The result demonstrated that the M-CDs exhibited marvelous reversibility and restorability whenever the temperature changed. In other words, the temperature (10–90°C) cannot cause permanent destruction on the surface fluorescent structure of the M-CDs. The decrease phenomenon may attributed to that the aggregation of M-CDs occurred which caused the obvious fluorescence quenching [16]. As seen in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs decreased monotonously with increasing temperature. There is a very good linear relationship between the intensity and temperature, which can be fitted as a function of
(2)
F =227.6389–1.253T (T: 10°C–90°C)
with correlation coefficient 0.9842, where F is the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs, T represents the temperature of the M-CDs system (°C). The relative sensitivity is usually utilized and defined as
(3)
S =
∂
( P )
/ ∂ T
P
where P is the measured temperature-sensitive parameter, such as intensity, lifetime, or intensity ratio [40,41]. For fluorescent thermometry, the relative sensitivity can be determined as
(4)
S =
∂
( F )
/ ∂ T
F
where F is the fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs. Following the definition, the maximum sensitivity of M-CDs is determined to be 1.09%°C−1 at 90°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of M-CDs to Fe3+ and fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of various metal ions on M-CDs was investigated. Different metal ions including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ of each at the concentration of 0.40mM were reacted with M-CDs solution (0.30mg/mL). As shown in Fig. 4 , the result revealed that the M-CDs were highly selective toward Fe3+. While, other metal ions showed either no or slight changed in the fluorescence intensity relative to the free M-CDs. Similarly, the competition experiments were carried out by adding 0.40mM Fe3+ to the solution of M-CDs (0.30mg/mL) in the presence of 0.40mM of the other metal ions; the fluorescence intensity of M-CDs showed no obviously change after mixed with any one of the interference metal ions. These results indicated that M-CDs were highly selective for the detection of Fe3+.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism by Fe3+ was investigated in some detail. XPS and FTIR analysis results revealed that M-CDs surface was covered with carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that the M-CDs response to Fe3+ is due to the strong affinity interaction and faster chelating kinetics between Fe3+ and carboxyl group and hydroxyl group distributing on the surface of the M-CDs than other metal ions [42]. Besides, Fe3+ was known to be able to absorb on the surface of M-CDs [43] and coordinate with carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups on edge of carbon dots, which result in the severe aggregation of M-CDs with each other. Meanwhile, due to this coordination interaction, electron in the excited state of M-CDs transfer to the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ and lead to nonradiative electron/hole recombination, which caused significant fluorescence quenching [1]. The proposed mechanism of aggregation caused fluorescence quenching by ferric ions in conformity with the TEM results.
Taking into account response results, schematic representation of synthetic procedure of M-CDs for Fe3+ ions detections and cells imaging was shown in Scheme 1 .

The M-CDs were synthesized by a simple calcined method, using mangosteen pulp as carbon source, without the assistance of any chemical reagent. The prepared M-CDs show strong blue fluorescence, which was quenched by Fe3+ ions, formed the M-CDs/Fe3+ complex. Meanwhile, the M-CDs can be used for cell imaging.

In an attempt to evaluate the influence of Fe3+concentration, firstly, the effect of reaction time on the detection of Fe3+ was also evaluated, and the results shown in Fig. S4. It can be seen that the reaction was rapid and finished within 2min. Furthermore, the different concentrations of Fe3+ were added to the solution of M-CDs and their corresponding fluorescence emission spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when no adding Fe3+ into the solution of M-CDs, the M-CDs displayed strong blue fluorescence at 440nm (Φ 1 =0.12). As expected, the fluorescence of M-CDs was quenched gradually with the increasing concentration of Fe3+ by more than 72% at 440nm (Φ 2 =0.02), revealing that the M-CDs were sensitive to Fe3+. The inset of Fig. 5a showed the quenching fluorescence at 440nm had a linear diminishment (linearly dependent coefficient: R 2 =0.9802) toward Fe3+ over the concentration range from 0 to 0.18mM. The limit of detection for Fe3+ was 52nM (3σ/K). This value is comparable to those in the literature, as listed in Table S1, suggesting higher sensitivity of this sensing system. Fig. 5b displayed the Stern-Volmer plot of M-CDs with increasing concentration of Fe3+ (F 0/F against concentration of Fe3+), where F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of M-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The quenching efficiency was fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(5)
F 0/F =1+ K sv[Q]
Where K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the Fe3+ concentration. The correlation coefficient (R 2) was 0.9983 over the concentration range of 0–0.28mM, indicating their excellent sensing properties in the detection of trace Fe3+and K sv was 6.5×103 L/mol.

The M-CDs were introduced into the yeast cells to show their bioimaging capabilities using a fluorescent microscopy test. As shown in Fig. 6 b, after incubation with M-CDs for 1h at 37°C, yeast cells showed strong blue fluorescence, indicating that the obtained M-CDs easily penetrated into the cells. To explore the effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence imaging of the cells, 0.40mM Fe3+ was mixed with the fluorescence-labeled cells and incubated for 30min at 37°C. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence of cell was drastically quenched. These results revealed that the M-CDs have the potential for bioimaging in yeast cells.

In summary, the M-CDs were successfully synthesized by simple one-step calcined mangosteen pulp in air without any further modification. The M-CDs exhibit good water solubility, highly fluorescent, and good photostability. Meanwhile, the M-CDs show excellent selectivity and sensitivity and were used as fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 52nM. In addition, the M-CDs also showed good biocompatibility and was used for the fluorescence imaging of yeast cells. These results demonstrate that the M-CDs may be used as an eco-friendly material, which will have great potential in biological and environmental application in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21176125) and the Science Research Project of the Ministry of Education of Heilongjiang Province of China (135109101).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.05.252.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging

Recent advances in bioapplications of C-dots

A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water- soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Carbon nanoparticles as visible-light photocatalysts for efficient CO2 conversion and beyond
Green synthesis of carbon dots from ocimum sanctum for effective fluorescent sensing of Pb2+ ions and live cell imaging
A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid

Dual-emissive nanohybrid of carbon dots and gold nanoclusters for sensitive determination of mercuric ions
A simple turn on fluorescent sensor for the selective detection of thiamine using coconut water derived luminescent carbon dots
Facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots and application for Fe(III) ions detection and cell imaging
Liquid nitrogen-assisted synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from blueberry and their performance in Fe3+ detection
Green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp
Novel and green synthesis of high-fluorescent carbon dots originated from honey for sensing and imaging
Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg(II) ions detection and cell imaging

Dual functional N- and S-co-doped carbon dots as the sensor for temperature and Fe3+ ions
Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli
Carbon dots preparation as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of Fe(III) ions in biological systems
Electrolyzing synthesis of boron-doped graphene quantum dots for fluorescence determination of Fe3+ ions in water samples
A general sensing strategy for detection of Fe3+ by using amino acid-modified graphene quantum dots as fluorescent probe
Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: Synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging
Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging
Determination of sunset yellow in soft drinks based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots
A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots
Naked oats-derived dual-emission carbon nanodots for ratiometric sensing and cellular imaging

Facile synthesis of water-soluble and biocompatible fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Investigating the surface state of grapheme quantum dots
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Dual-emitting MOF⊃dye composite for ratiometric temperature sensing
Ratiometric nanothermometer based on an emissive Ln3+-organic framework
Polymer nanodots of graphitic carbon nitride as effective fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
